# Last Time

::: {.incremental}
+ **Nested Loops**
+ **Multi-Dimensional Vectors**
:::

::: notes
Recall how nested loops coordinate repeated work and how multi-dimensional vectors organize data by row and column.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

# Today's Agenda

- Stuff To Do With A Loop
- Drawing Graphs Using Loops
- Menu-Driven Programming

::: notes

:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

{{< include topic_1_loop_patterns.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_2_drawing_graphs.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_3_menu_driven_programming.qmd >}}

---

# Key Takeaways

- Loop patterns name the problem before the syntax.
- Terminal graphs are repeated output with labels, scale, and structure.
- Menu-driven programs use loops to keep interaction going.

<!-- Slide 50 -->

---

# Next Time

- Input validation with loops
- Specific error messages
- Reprompting until input is usable

<!-- Slide 51 -->

---

# Appendix

<!-- Slide 52 -->

---

## Stuff To Do With A Loop: Good Practices

- Name the purpose of the loop before writing it.
- Initialize counters, totals, flags, and trackers before the loop.
- Make the loop move toward stopping.

<!-- Slide 53 -->

---

## Stuff To Do With A Loop: Common Mistakes

- Updating a counter in the wrong place can change how many times the loop runs.
- Processing a sentinel value as real data changes the result.
- Initializing a max or min tracker to an arbitrary value can hide real data.

<!-- Slide 54 -->

---

## Stuff To Do With A Loop: More Examples

```cpp
int total = 0;
int count = 0;
int value;

while (cin >> value) {
    total += value;
    count++;
}
```

This loop combines a running total with a counter.

<!-- Slide 55 -->

---

## Stuff To Do With A Loop: Practice Problems

- Count how many entered numbers are positive.
- Add sales values until the client enters `-1`.
- Find the largest of five quiz scores.

<!-- Slide 56 -->

---

## Stuff To Do With A Loop: AI Search Terms

- C++ counter loop examples
- C++ sentinel value loop
- C++ running total accumulator
- C++ max tracker loop

<!-- Slide 57 -->

---

## Stuff To Do With A Loop: Questions for Reflection

- What value changes each pass?
- What condition stops the loop?
- What pattern best describes the loop's purpose?

<!-- Slide 58 -->

---

## Drawing Graphs Using Loops: Good Practices

- Decide what comparison the graph should show.
- Keep labels aligned and symbols consistent.
- Scale large values so the graph fits the screen.

<!-- Slide 59 -->

---

## Drawing Graphs Using Loops: Common Mistakes

- Printing raw values can make bars too long.
- Leaving off labels makes the graph hard to read.
- Mixing symbols can make the output look accidental.

<!-- Slide 60 -->

---

## Drawing Graphs Using Loops: More Examples

```cpp
for (int row = 1; row <= 5; row++) {
    cout << row << " ";
    for (int mark = 1; mark <= row; mark++) {
        cout << '*';
    }
    cout << endl;
}
```

This graph grows one symbol longer on each row.

<!-- Slide 61 -->

---

## Drawing Graphs Using Loops: Practice Problems

- Draw a bar for each day of the week.
- Scale miles traveled so one `#` represents ten miles.
- Print a triangle with five rows.

<!-- Slide 62 -->

---

## Drawing Graphs Using Loops: AI Search Terms

- C++ terminal bar chart loop
- C++ nested loops pattern output
- C++ console graph scale factor
- C++ print histogram with loops

<!-- Slide 63 -->

---

## Drawing Graphs Using Loops: Questions for Reflection

- What does each symbol represent?
- Which loop prints rows?
- Which loop prints repeated symbols within a row?

<!-- Slide 64 -->

---

## Menu-Driven Programming: Good Practices

- Use a clear quit option.
- Keep the menu cycle easy to read.
- Move larger actions into functions.

<!-- Slide 65 -->

---

## Menu-Driven Programming: Common Mistakes

- Forgetting `break` in a `switch` can run the next case.
- Mixing menu display and action code makes the program hard to change.
- A menu with no quit path traps the client.

<!-- Slide 66 -->

---

## Menu-Driven Programming: More Examples

```cpp
char choice;

do {
    cout << "A. Add\nD. Display\nQ. Quit\n";
    cin >> choice;

    switch (choice) {
    case 'A':
        cout << "Add" << endl;
        break;
    case 'D':
        cout << "Display" << endl;
        break;
    }
} while (choice != 'Q');
```

<!-- Slide 67 -->

---

## Menu-Driven Programming: Practice Problems

- Build a menu with options to add, display, and quit.
- Rewrite a numeric menu as a letter menu.
- Move each menu action into its own function.

<!-- Slide 68 -->

---

## Menu-Driven Programming: AI Search Terms

- C++ do while menu
- C++ switch menu example
- C++ menu driven program functions
- C++ numeric vs letter menu

<!-- Slide 69 -->

---

## Menu-Driven Programming: Questions for Reflection

- What choice stops the menu?
- Which part of the code dispatches the action?
- What data should persist between menu choices?

<!-- Slide 70 -->
